post_content: At the beginning of the year, we ran into a good problem. We were closing great clients, but getting them from “yes” to signed was taking too long. Our contract was 36 pages—built from seven years of lessons, edge cases, and hard-earned experience. It protected the business, but it also created friction. Even committed clients were taking 72 hours or more to sign. In one of our meetings, Timothy Springer helped us rethink the experience. We kept the substance of the agreement, but simplified how it was presented: a clean order form connected to a Master Services Agreement. This created far less friction. Since making the change, our average signature time has dropped from 72 hours or more to less than 24 (in some cases). The lesson for me: as a business evolves, complexity naturally accumulates. But sophistication is not the complexity itself. It is knowing how to preserve everything you have learned while making the experience simpler for the customer. Sometimes the next stage of growth comes from removing the distance between a decision and action. It also happens to be your birthday today, Tim. Thank you for all that you do for us! I have never learned so much from someone in 12 months. 1 to 100 hashtag #SpringSteps …more

A classroom full of kids chanting your name. Not in the way you'd want. That was seventh grade for Derek Coburn. Teachers telling him he'd never be successful. ADHD. Bad grades. Sneaking out of school to run a comic book business. Most people would have made that their story. Derek made it his fuel. His whole life became one long "you don't think I can? Watch me." And for decades, that energy ran quietly in the background — powering every risk, every bet, every client he won. He didn't even realize it until his midlife crisis a few years ago. That's the part worth sitting with. A lot of the most driven people you know are running on pain they've never examined. It works — until you realize the scoreboard you've been chasing was never really yours. What's the "watch me" moment that's been quietly driving you? 👇 …more

For the past few years, I’ve been going through a pretty major health journey. One of the things I haven’t been able to do is walk long distances. And yes, I’m 33. But sometimes, no matter your age, you have to reset the foundation your body is built on. The past 9 months, I’ve finally started making real progress. Slowly, carefully, and with a lot more appreciation than I had before, I’m getting back to one of the activities I love most: hiking. So I’m excited to share that I’ve begun organizing a hiking group for the Bay Area community alongside Tribe, with the incredible support of Kirk Gentiles, Peter Lam, and Rajat Suri. The goal is simple: Bring together founders, operators, and leaders for something that blends movement, fresh air, thoughtful conversation, and community. Our next hike is Saturday, July 18 at Sawyer Camp Trail. If you’re in the Bay Area and want to join a group of smart, curious, good people for a morning outside, come hike with us. https://lnkd.in/gjgf8SSk …more

A former Apple designer built Ferrari's first electric car. It was flawless. And everyone hated it. Not because it was ugly — because it didn't feel like anything. No noise. No tension. No danger. Just restraint, borrowed from a brand that made restraint a religion. That's the trap right now with AI. Everyone's asking it to hand them a personality. A voice. A "brand." As if taste is something you can prompt your way into. It's not. Taste is autobiography. It's your dad ripping out tile because "good enough" offended him. It's your mom teaching refugees they deserve to be understood before they're judged. It's every room you sat in where you were the outsider, quietly taking notes. AI has access to everything ever made. What it doesn't have is a life that made it who it is. You do. That's the entire advantage. Stop asking the machine who you should be. Full breakdown (with Eli Wright, our Head of Tastemaking) in the newsletter 👇 …more
